theme: The office has secrets — reward the curious, animate the mundane, and let no element be silent

ideas.title: Mobile Tap Bottom-Sheet (Character Dossier)
ideas.description: On mobile/touch devices, tapping a character in the Office floor currently does nothing visible (desktop click routes to Dashboard, but touch users never get a payoff). Implement a swipe-up bottom-sheet that overlays the bottom 55% of the viewport on tap: large CharacterAvatar (56px), name + role badge, current status + 'Working for Xm' duration, task text, and their last 3 activityLog entries as a minimal timeline. Dismiss on backdrop-tap or swipe-down gesture. Detect touch via `window.matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')` or `ontouchstart` and branch the `onWorkerClick` handler — desktop keeps its dashboard-jump behavior, touch gets the sheet. The sheet needs z-index above the office floor, a semi-transparent backdrop, and a `border-radius: 24px 24px 0 0` entrance animation from the bottom (transform translateY). WHY: On mobile, the office is where most casual observers land — they see little avatars and naturally tap them. Right now that curiosity hits a wall. The bottom-sheet turns a dead interaction into the primary mobile storytelling moment without disrupting desktop UX.

ideas.title: Keyboard Shortcut Discovery Toast
ideas.description: The app has 4 keyboard shortcuts (1 = Office, 2 = Dashboard, R = Refresh, Esc = Blur) that are completely invisible to users. On first visit (gate with `localStorage.getItem('bloberto-shortcuts-shown')`), show a floating toast in the bottom-right corner after a 1.5s delay: '⌨️ Shortcuts: 1 Office · 2 Dashboard · R Refresh · Esc Clear'. Style it like a terminal chip — monospace font, dark surface, subtle border, 80% opacity. Auto-dismiss after 7s with a fade-out. On first keypress of any shortcut key, dismiss immediately with a 'Got it ✓' flash. Set localStorage flag on dismiss. WHY: Power features that no one discovers might as well not exist. This toast converts hidden functionality into actual muscle memory for return visitors. It's 20 lines of code and zero dependencies.

ideas.title: The Door Animates on Worker Arrivals & Exits
ideas.description: The `office-door` div (bottom center, '🚪 Entrance') is purely decorative — a static emoji and label. It should react to worker events that already fire in App.jsx: when `newIds` gains a new entry (worker arrived), the door plays a 'swing open' CSS keyframe (rotateY 0→-35deg and back, 1.5s ease). When `fadingMap` gains a new entry (worker leaving), play a 'swing out' variant (rotateY 0→-25deg, slower, 2s). Pass a boolean prop like `isDoorOpen` or event-count to Office.jsx, triggered by changes to `newIds.size` and `Object.keys(fadingMap).length`. The door emoji can also briefly change to '🚪✨' on entry. Add a tiny 'ping' notification dot above the door during transition. WHY: Worker arrivals and exits are significant narrative events — the confetti burst fires in the header (far away) but the office floor has no physical reaction at the point of entry. The door is the single most obvious place to anchor that event. Closing this loop makes the floor feel physically connected to the real world.

ideas.title: Luna's Permanent OOO Sticky on the Whiteboard
ideas.description: Luna (the Creative Director) is in `DEFAULT_ROSTER` but never appears as an active worker — she's perpetually a ghost, always reviewing remotely. Right now she's just absent from the floor with zero acknowledgment. Lean into the lore: render a small sticky note SVG pinned to the bottom-right corner of the whiteboard element (or just below it, left wall area). When Luna is NOT in the active workers array (her perpetual state), show the sticky: '🌙 Luna is reviewing remotely. This is intentional.' plus the relative timestamp of her last activityLog entry ('Last seen: 2h ago'). If Luna IS ever active, hide the sticky and render her on the floor normally. The note should use the same `desk-away-sign` visual language — yellow sticky, slight rotation, handwritten-style font. WHY: Luna's invisibility is currently a plot hole. The sticky note transforms it into a recurring in-universe joke and makes the Creative Director's role legible to anyone watching. It also closes the 'where's Luna?' question that any attentive observer would ask.

ideas.title: Dashboard Duration Heatmap Bar
ideas.description: Add a horizontal 'time-in-state' visualization between the StatsBar and the worker grid on the Dashboard tab. One row per active worker: left-pinned label (emoji + name), then a horizontal bar whose width is proportional to time elapsed since `updated_at` (cap at 4 hours = 100% width). Color: working = role color with low opacity glow; idle = grey; error = red pulse. Inside each bar, show a right-aligned text label: 'Working 1h 20m' or 'Idle 45m'. Sort rows: errors first, then working (longest first), then idle. All data is already on the workers array — no new fetch needed. WHY: The StatsBar shows COUNTS. Worker cards show CURRENT STATE. Neither answers 'who's been at it the longest?' or 'is anyone overdue for a break?' The duration bar gives that at a glance. It transforms the Dashboard from a status list into a temporal picture of the workday.

ideas.title: Bloberto's 9AM Standup Note
ideas.description: Every day between 9:00–9:59 AM local time (checked via `new Date().getHours() === 9`), slide a folded sticky note out from behind the whiteboard into the office floor. The note is assembled from activityLog entries over the past 12 hours using a short template: 'Yesterday: [last complete event + worker]. Today: [N] workers online. Blockers: [last error task, or 'none']. — B 🫠'. The note uses a slightly larger sticky SVG (40×50px), pinned to the bottom edge of the whiteboard. Add a matching indicator in the StatsBar: a tiny '📋 standup up' badge that links/scrolls to the whiteboard when clicked. At 10:00 AM, the note fades back behind the whiteboard. On initial load, check the hour and display immediately if still in window. No new data fetch — synthesize from existing activityLog state. WHY: Standup is the most universal ritual in software teams. Bloberto generating his own daily standup from actual data is pure lore and it gives the site a concrete reason to check back at 9am.

ideas.title: Footer Version Badge → Micro-Changelog Popover
ideas.description: The footer currently shows 'v1.0.0-chaos' as static text. Make it a clickable chip: on click, open a small popover above it (tooltip-style, max-width 280px, semi-transparent dark surface) listing the last 5 'complete' or 'hire' type entries from activityLog as a pseudo-changelog: '✅ Maya shipped X · 2h ago', '🟢 Carlos onboarded · 1d ago'. Title it '📦 Recent Shipments'. Dismiss on click-outside. This requires zero new data — it's just a filtered view of the existing activityLog already in state. WHY: The footer is wasted space. Converting the version badge into a live changelog popover is a 2-line-of-data trick that makes the footer the most secretly useful spot on the page. It also normalizes checking the activity log for users who don't navigate to the Dashboard tab.

ideas.title: Achievement Badge Pins on Bloberto's Desk
ideas.description: As the observer watches the office over time, Bloberto's manager desk nameplate area unlocks micro-achievement badge pins — tiny 10px circle glyphs rendered in SVG next to the nameplate. Achievements earned and persisted in `localStorage` under 'bloberto-achievements': 🏅 MARATHON (any worker active 4+ consecutive hours — detectable from `updated_at` delta), 🏅 GHOST TOWN (all workers idle simultaneously — derived from vibe='slow-day' with 0 working), 🏅 HOTFIX HERO (any worker transitions error→working within 5 min — tracked via prev/current worker status diff in the polling diff logic already in App.jsx), 🏅 PERFECT SYNC (all workers simultaneously 'working'), 🏅 NIGHT OWL (any worker active after 22:00 local). Badges render as tiny pins left of the nameplate. Hover on any pin: Bloberto shows a speech bubble with the badge name + a dry Bloberto-voice description. New badge unlock: play the same confetti burst used for vibe improvements + brief badge-pop animation. WHY: Achievements are self-documenting event memory. They make rare states — things observers usually miss — into collectible milestones. They incentivize leaving the tab open and returning, and they quietly document Bloberto's team lore in a way no log ever could.
